elements.sent-2411-004: We obtain formulae for the minimum transformation degrees of the most well-studied families of finite diagram monoids, including the partition, Brauer, Temperley--Lieb and Motzkin monoids.
elements.sent-2411-005: For example, the partition monoid $\mathcal P_n$ has degree $1 + \frac{B(n+2)-B(n+1)+B(n) 2$ for $n\geq2$, where these are Bell numbers.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The proofs involve constructing explicit faithful representations of the minimum degree, many of which can be realised as (partial) actions on projections.
elements.sent-2411-007: Keywords: Diagram monoids, transformation representations, transformation degrees.
elements.sent-2411-009: The minimum transformation degree of a finite monoid $M$, denoted $\deg(M)$, is the least integer~${n\geq1}$ such that $M$ can be faithfully represented by transformations (self-maps) of a set of size~$n$.
elements.sent-2411-010: This is well defined because of Cayley's Theorem, which says that $M$ acts faithfully on itself by translations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Much of the initial work on transformation degrees was undertaken by Easdown and Schein \cite{Easdown1987,Easdown1988,Easdown1992,Schein1988,Schein1992 , but see also \cite{Stoll1944,Tully1961,Slover1965,Hoehnke1963 for some earlier work on transformation representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: See also \cite{EP1988,EH2016,EST2010,HW2002,Johnson1971,KP2000,Saunders2010,Wright1975 for group-theoretic investigations, and \cite{BT1991,EEMP2019,EEM2017,Holt2010,ACMT2023, LPRR2002,LPRR1998,LM1990,FP1997 for connections to computational algebra.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The current article concerns the class of diagram monoids, which consist of various kinds of set partitions that are depicted and multiplied diagrammatically.
elements.sent-2411-015: These monoids, and related algebras and categories built from them, have origins and applications in many fields of mathematics and science \cite{Brauer1937,BH2014,Jones1994_2,Martin1994,TL1971 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The diagram monoids we consider here are all submonoids of the partition monoid $\P_n$.
elements.sent-2411-017: This monoid, which will be defined below, contains the full transformation monoid $\T_n$, as well as its opposite monoid $\T_n\opp$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Margolis and Steinberg have recently shown \cite{MS2023 that $\deg(\T_n\opp) = 2^n$, and this leads to the lower bound of $\deg(\P_n)\geq2^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-019: As far as we are aware, no upper bound for $\deg(\P_n)$ has been given in the literature, apart from $\deg(\P_n)\leq|\P_n|$, from Cayley's Theorem.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, since $\P_n$ is fundamental \cite{EMRT2018 , it follows from \cite[Theorem 5]{Imaoka1980 that $\P_n$ can be faithfully represented in $\T_P\times\T_P\opp$, where $P = P(\P_n)$ is the set of projections of~$\P_n$ (again, see below for the precise definitions).}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Combining this with the result from \cite{MS2023 mentioned above, this leads to the upper bound of $\deg(\P_n) \leq |P| + 2^{|P|}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: For example, when $n=3$ these bounds are: \begin{equation}8 \leq \deg(\P_3) \leq 4194\ 326.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-023: This upper bound is not an improvement on Cayley's Theorem, which gives ${\deg(\P_3) \leq |\P_3| = 203}$.
elements.sent-2411-024: As we will see, the exact value of $\deg(\P_3)$ turns out to be $22$.
elements.sent-2411-025: One our main results here is that in fact $\deg(\P_n) = |Q|+1$, where $Q\sub P$ is the set of projections of rank at most~$2$.
elements.sent-2411-026: A combinatorial argument then leads to the concise formula \begin{equation}\deg(\P_n) = 1 + \frac{B(n+2) - B(n+1) + B(n)}2,\end{equation} in terms of Bell numbers.
elements.sent-2411-027: The `$1+$' in the above formula can be removed to obtain the minimum partial transformation degree, $\deg'(\P_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-028: We also obtain analogous results for several other important families of diagram monoids---namely the Brauer monoid $\B_n$, the partial Brauer monoid $\PB_n$, the planar partition monoid~$\PP_n$, the Motzkin monoid $\M_n$, and the Temperley--Lieb monoid $\TL_n$---in terms of equally-natural number sequences, such as Catalan and Motzkin numbers.
elements.sent-2411-029: These formulae (stated for partial transformation degrees) are summarised in Table \ref{tab:Q1 ; calculated values are given in~Table~\ref{tab:Q2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: The paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: We begin in Section \ref{sect:prelim with preliminaries on semigroups and diagram monoids.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sect:T contains general results on transformation representations, and their connections to actions and right congruences.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Sections \ref{sect:P and \ref{sect:TL then apply this to the monoids $\P_n$, $\PB_n$, $\PP_n$, $\M_n$ and $\TL_n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The main results here are Theorems~\ref{thm:M and \ref{thm:TL , which show that the degree of each such monoid is $1 + |Q|$, for a suitable set $Q$ of low-rank projections.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Brauer monoid $\B_n$ is treated in Section~\ref{sect:B , where we must use rather different methods; see Theorem~\ref{thm:B .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: En route to proving these theorems, we construct explicit faithful actions/representations of the stated degree; see Theorems~\ref{thm:P ,~\ref{thm:M1 ,~\ref{thm:Bnodd and~\ref{thm:Bneven .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Since every such action contains a global fixed point, the minimum partial transformation degree is given by $\deg'(M) = \deg(M) - 1$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Finally, in Section~\ref{sect:C we give combinatorial formulae for~$|Q|$ for each the monoids~$\P_n$,~$\PB_n$,~$\PP_n$,~$\M_n$ and~$\TL_n$.. {\bf Acknowledgements.}}

elements.sent-2411-041: We begin by recalling the preliminary ideas and results we need concerning semigroups (Section~\ref{subsect:M ), regular $*$-semigroups (Section \ref{subsect:RSS ) and diagram monoids (Section \ref{subsect:D ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: For more basic background on semigroup theory, see for example \cite{Howie1995,CPbook .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Let $S$ be a semigroup, and let $S^1$ be the monoid completion of $S$.
elements.sent-2411-044: So $S^1=S$ if $S$ is a monoid, or else $S=S\cup\{1\}$, where $1$ is a symbol not belonging to $S$, acting as an adjoined identity element.
elements.sent-2411-045: Green's $\L$, $\R$ and $\J$ pre-orders and equivalences are defined, for $a,b\in S$, by \begin{equation}a\leqL b \iff a\in S^1b , a\mr\L b \iff S^1a=S^1b, a \leqR b \iff a\in bS^1, a \mr\R b \iff aS^1= bS^1, a \leqJ b \iff a\in S^1bS^1, a \mr\J b \iff S^1aS^1= S^1bS^1.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}a\leqL b \iff a\in S^1b , a\mr\L b \iff S^1a=S^1b, a \leqR b \iff a\in bS^1, a \mr\R b \iff aS^1= bS^1, a \leqJ b \iff a\in S^1bS^1, a \mr\J b \iff S^1aS^1= S^1bS^1.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: So, for example, $a\mr\L b$ holds when either $a=b$, or else $a=sb$ and $b=ta$ for some $s,t\in S$; similar comments apply to $\R$ and $\J$.
elements.sent-2411-047: We also have the $\H$ and~$\D$ relations, defined by $\H=\L\cap\R$ and $\D=\L\vee\R$, where the latter is the join of $\L$ and $\R$ in the lattice of all equivalences of~$S$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The $\R$-class of an element $a\in S$ is denoted by $R_a$, and similarly for $\L$-classes, etc. The set~$S/\R$ of all $\R$-classes is partially ordered by \begin{equation}\label{eq:leqR} R_a\leq R_b \iff a\leqR b \qquad\text{for $a,b\in S$.}\end{equation} A semigroup is stable if \begin{equation}\label{eq:stab} sa \mr\J a \iff sa \mr\L a \AND as \mr\J a \iff as\mr\R a \qquad\text{for all $a,s\in S$.}\end{equation} Any finite semigroup is stable.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:stab} sa \mr\J a \iff sa \mr\L a \AND as \mr\J a \iff as\mr\R a \qquadfor all $a,s\in S$.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-050: An equivalence relation $\si$ on a semigroup $S$ is a right congruence if it is right-compatible, meaning that \begin{equation}(a,b)\in\si \implies (as,bs)\in\si \qquadfor all $a,b,s\in S$.\end{equation} Left congruences are defined symmetrically.
elements.sent-2411-051: A (two-sided) congruence is an equivalence that is both a left and right congruence.
elements.sent-2411-052: For example, $\L$ is a right congruence, and $\R$ is a left congruence.
elements.sent-2411-053: The trivial and universal congruences are respectively denoted \begin{equation}\De_S = \set{(a,a)}{a\in S} \AND \nab_S = S\times S.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: For a set of pairs $\Si\sub S\times S$, we write $\Si^\sharp$ for the (two-sided) congruence of $S$ generated by~$\Si$, i.e.~the intersection of all congruences containing $\Si$.
elements.sent-2411-055: When $\Si=\{(a,b)\}$ for some $a,b\in S$, we write $(a,b)^\sharp = \Si^\sharp$; such a congruence is called principal.
elements.sent-2411-056: A right ideal of a semigroup $S$ is a subset $I\sub S$ such that $IS\sub I$.
elements.sent-2411-057: Left ideals and (two-sided) ideals are defined analogously.
elements.sent-2411-058: Any left, right or two-sided ideal is a union of $\L$-, $\R$- or $\J$-classes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: If $I$ is a right ideal, then we have the Rees right congruence \begin{equation}\RR_I = \nab_I \cup \De_S = \set{(x,y)\in S\times S}{x=y  or  x,y\in I}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: As special cases we have $\RR_\es = \De_S$ and $\RR_S = \nab_S$.
elements.sent-2411-061: A regular $*$-semigroup is a semigroup $S$ with an additional unary operation $S\to S:a\mt a^*$ satisfying the identities \begin{equation}a^{**}=a = aa^*a \AND (ab)^*=b^*a^* \qquadfor all $a,b\in S$.\end{equation} These were introduced by Nordahl and Scheiblich in \cite{NS1978 , and the diagram monoids considered here are key examples.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The set of projections of a regular $*$-semigroup $S$ is denoted \begin{equation}P(S) = \set{p\in S}{p^2=p=p^*}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-063: It is well known (see for example \cite{Petrich1985 ) that $P(S) = \set{aa^* {a\in S = \set{a^*a {a\in S}$, and that \begin{equation}\label{eq:LR*} a\mr\L b \iff a^*a=b^*b \AND a\mr\R b \iff aa^*=bb^* \qquad\text{for all $a,b\in S$.}\end{equation} Since every element of $S$ is $\R$-related to a unique projection (namely $a\mr\R aa^*$), with a similar statement for the $\L$ relation, it follows that any $\D$-class $D$ of $S$ contains $|D/\L| = |D/\R|$ projections, and that $|P(S)| = |S/\L| = |S/\R|$.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-064: Fix a non-negative integer $n\geq0$, and write $\bn=\{1,\ldots,n\}$ and $\bn'=\{1',\ldots,n'\}$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The partition monoid~$\P_n$ is the set of all set partitions of $\bn\cup\bn'$.
elements.sent-2411-066: Such a partition is identified with any graph on vertex set $\bn\cup\bn'$ whose connected components are the blocks of the partition; the vertices from $\bn$ and~$\bn'$ are drawn on an upper and lower row, respectively, ordered $1<\cdots<n$ and~$1'<\cdots<n'$.
elements.sent-2411-067: The product of $\al,\be\in\P_n$ is defined as follows.
elements.sent-2411-068: We first write $\bn''=\{1'',\ldots,n''\}$, and we let: \bit \item $\al^\vee$ be the graph on vertex set $\bn\cup\bn''$ obtained by changing each lower vertex $x'$ of $\al$ to $x''$, \item $\be^\wedge$ be the graph on vertex set $\bn''\cup\bn'$ obtained by changing each upper vertex $x$ of $\be$ to~$x''$, \item $\Pi(\al,\be)$ be the graph on vertex set $\bn\cup\bn''\cup\bn'$ whose edge set is the union of the edge sets of $\al^\vee$ and $\be^\wedge$.
elements.sent-2411-069: The product $\al\be\in\P_n$ is then the unique partition of $\bn\cup\bn'$ such that $x,y\in\bn\cup\bn'$ belong to the same block of $\al\be$ if and only if they belong to the same connected component of~$\Pi(\al,\be)$.
elements.sent-2411-070: An example calculation is given in Figure \ref{fig:P6 in the case $n=6$.}

elements.sent-2411-088: For a set $X$, the full transformation monoid $\T_X$ consists of all self-maps of $X$ under composition.

elements.sent-2411-565: The study of permutation groups is as old as group theory itself, going back to the work of Galois on polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-566: In a sense, group theory is the study of permutation groups; indeed, as soon as Cayley gave the axioms of a group, he showed that every abstract group $G$ embeds in the symmetric group $\S_G$.
elements.sent-2411-567: In the standard embedding, each $g\in G$ is mapped to the right translation $\rho_g:G\to G$, acting by $a\rho_g = ag$ for all $a\in G$.
elements.sent-2411-568: Thus, when $G$ is finite, it embeds in a finite symmetric group~$\S_n$.
elements.sent-2411-569: The smallest such $n$ is called the (minimum permutation) degree of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-570: This invariant is notoriously difficult to calculate, even for fairly simple groups, and can interact unintuitively with basic constructions such as (semi)direct products and quotients; see for example \cite{EP1988,EH2016,EST2010,HW2002,Johnson1971,KP2000,Saunders2010,Wright1975, Saunders2014,KP1989,BSS2017,OPU2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-571: The proof of Cayley's Theorem works without modification to show that any finite monoid~$M$ (and hence any finite semigroup) embeds in a finite full transformation monoid $\T_n$, which consists of all self-maps of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-572: The smallest such $n$ is called the (minimum transformation) degree of $M$, and is denoted~$\deg(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-573: Much of the initial work on transformation degrees was undertaken by Easdown and Schein \cite{Easdown1987,Easdown1988,Easdown1992,Schein1988,Schein1992 ; for some earlier work on transformation representations see \cite{Stoll1944,Tully1961,Slover1965,Hoehnke1963 .}}

elements.sent-2411-575: It can again be difficult to calculate the degree of a given semigroup, even in very simple cases, and there are very few papers in the literature on this topic.
